title: Have the Fundamentalists Won?
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Have the Fundamentalists Won_ - Pastor Tito Lyro - Matthew 16_13-23.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The 100th Anniversary of Fosdick's Sermon
subpoints: On May 21, 1922, Harry Emerson Fosdick preached 'Shall the Fundamentalist Win?' at First Presbyterian Church, New York — a sermon that shaped American Protestantism for a century
Fosdick's fundamentalists were not legalists concerned with skirt lengths, but people who held the Bible as the highest authority for faith and life
Rockefeller funded Riverside Church as a platform for Fosdick's liberal teaching after the sermon led to his removal from the Presbyterian pulpit
100 years later: have the fundamentalists won? To answer, we turn first to Scripture and then briefly to the book of Providence

outline.title: What Is a Win? Four Answers to 'Who Is Jesus?'
subpoints: Jesus poses the most important question humanity faces: 'Who do men say that I, the Son of Man, am?' (v. 13) — a win is a church built on the solid rock of the correct answer
Answer 1 — An imposter: the Pharisees' view; shared by Voltaire and Karl Marx; the foundation of communism is the claim that Jesus was a deceiver

Answer 2 — A great teacher: the view of Nicodemus initially, of Eastern religions, of the modern world, and of the PCUSA, UMC, and ELCA; but it is logically impossible — Jesus claimed to be more than a teacher, so he is either a liar, lunatic, or Lord
Answer 3 — A prophet: the masses' view in Jesus' time; paralleled today by Mormonism (an angel from God), Islam (a prophet of Allah), and Jehovah's Witnesses

Answer 4 — 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God' (v. 16): Peter's answer on behalf of the apostles, given after unparalleled intimate contact with Jesus — those who knew him best could only say this

outline.title: The Church Is Built on This Confession
subpoints: Verse 17: 'flesh and blood has not revealed this to you, but my Father who is in heaven' — this is a divinely inspired statement
Verse 18: 'on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades shall not prevail against it' — the church has a guaranteed future because it is Christ's project, not ours

Fosdick argued for an institution tolerant of all views concerning the Bible — but Jesus says: remove Christ as Savior and Scripture as Word of God, and whatever you have is not the church; it is the synagogue of Satan
The most offensive claim of Christianity: Jesus is not a way but the only way; not one among many options but the exclusive mediator — 'no one comes to the Father except through me' (John 14:6)

outline.title: The Church Built on Christ Cannot Be Defeated
subpoints: Hades (the underworld, standing for death and the dominion of Satan) has been defeated in the resurrection — 'death is swallowed up in victory; O death, where is your sting?' (1 Corinthians 15:54-57)
The religion of Fosdick and the liberals is not Christianity — without the Christ of the Bible, there is no Christianity
Fosdick's version of the church needed tolerance of all ideas; Christ's church is built on the exclusive confession that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the living God

outline.title: Providence: The Aftermath of the Modernist-Fundamentalist Controversy
subpoints: Hundreds of ministers lost pulpits and pensions; churches lost buildings they had occupied for over a century — because they believed Jesus is the Christ and Scripture is the Word of God
The Senate of Washington Presbytery (PCUSA) deposed Roy Brumba, first pastor of Tacoma Bible Presbyterian Church, in 1935 for refusing to support missionaries who denied the Bible and Jesus
In Collingswood, NJ, the congregation that became Collingswood Bible Presbyterian Church worshiped in a tent for months after losing their building

The daughter churches of the original fundamentalists have grown in the aggregate while remaining faithful to the Word of God; the PCUSA has seen membership and influence decline drastically year after year

Conclusion: as long as faithful men stand for the Word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ, the only possible answer is a resounding yes — the fundamentalists have won, because Jesus is building his church and the gates of hell will not prevail

summary:
Preached to mark the 100th anniversary of Harry Emerson Fosdick's 1922 sermon 'Shall the Fundamentalist Win?', Pastor Lyro turns the question around: a century later, have the fundamentalists won? The answer comes first from Matthew 16:13-23, where Jesus asks his disciples the most important question anyone will ever face — 'Who do you say that I am?' The sermon traces four possible answers given at the time of Jesus that still represent every position taken today: imposter (the Pharisees; Karl Marx); great teacher (Nicodemus; the mainline denominations; the modern world); prophet (the masses; Islam; Mormonism); and 'the Christ, the Son of the Living God' (the apostles, who knew Jesus best).

A win, the sermon argues, is a church built on that fourth answer — and on the divinely inspired recognition behind it. Jesus declares that on this truth he will build his church, and the gates of Hades shall not prevail. The liberalism that Fosdick championed insisted on tolerating all views concerning Christ and Scripture; Jesus says that without the Christ of the Bible and the Scripture as the Word of God, you do not have a church at all. The most offensive claim of Christianity is not its exclusivity of lifestyle but its exclusivity of salvation: Jesus is not a way but the only way.

The sermon then turns to Providence. In the aftermath of the fundamentalist-modernist controversy, faithful ministers lost pensions, churches lost their buildings of over a century, Roy Brumba of Tacoma was deposed for insisting missionaries affirm the Bible and Christ. A congregation in New Jersey worshiped in a tent. From the outside it looked like the fundamentalists lost. But the daughter churches of the original fundamentalists have steadily grown while remaining faithful, while the PCUSA has declined year after year. The verdict: as long as faithful people stand for the Word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ, the answer is a resounding yes.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Matthew 16:13-23

*When Jesus came into the region of Caesarea Philippi, He asked His disciples, saying, "Who do men say that I, the Son of Man, am?" So they said, "Some say John the Baptist, some Elijah, and others Jeremiah or one of the prophets." He said to them, "But who do you say that I am?" Simon Peter answered and said, "You are the Christ, the Son of the living God." Jesus answered and said to him, "Blessed are you, Simon Bar-Jonah, for flesh and blood has not revealed this to you, but My Father who is in heaven. And I also say to you that you are Peter, and on this rock I will build My church, and the gates of Hades shall not prevail against it. And I will give you the keys of the kingdom of heaven, and whatever you bind on earth will be bound in heaven, and whatever you loose on earth will be loosed in heaven." Then He commanded His disciples that they should tell no one that He was Jesus the Christ.*

*From that time Jesus began to show to His disciples that He must go to Jerusalem, and suffer many things from the elders and chief priests and scribes, and be killed, and be raised the third day. Then Peter took Him aside and began to rebuke Him, saying, "Far be it from You, Lord; this shall not happen to You!" But He turned and said to Peter, "Get behind Me, Satan! You are an offense to Me, for you are not mindful of the things of God, but the things of men."*

## Introduction: May 21, 1922

You may know what happened on May 21, 1942. But that is not the event we want to consider this morning. The event I want to build our sermon around took place on May 21, 1922 — one hundred years before. On that day, the celebrated Baptist preacher Harry Emerson Fosdick preached the infamous sermon titled "Shall the Fundamentalist Win?" at First Presbyterian Church in New York City.

This sermon led to his eventual removal from that pulpit — not on the basis of what he said, but because as a Baptist in a Presbyterian church, when someone complained, the Presbytery asked him to stop preaching there. Ironically, this sermon also gave him a new church, because as a reaction to it, John D. Rockefeller Jr. built Riverside Church — a massive building in New York City — so that Fosdick would have a pulpit to continue spreading his teaching.

Fosdick was concerned about the fundamentalists who were making noise in the PCUSA and among American Baptists. Now some of you hear the word "fundamentalist" and start sweating cold sweat. Maybe you were part of a church more concerned with the length of your skirt than the state of your soul — more concerned with outward appearance than with the heart. That is not the kind of fundamentalist we are talking about. The fundamentalism Fosdick opposed held the Bible as the highest authority for faith and living. He was worried that these people — people who really believed the Bible — were too backward, that they were disturbing the church about matters that didn't really matter.

This is what Fosdick says: *It is interesting to note where the fundamentalists are driving in their stakes to mark out the deadline of doctrine around the church, across which no one is to pass except on terms of agreement.* They insist, he says, that we must all believe in the historicity of the virgin birth of our Lord. That we must believe the original documents of Scripture were inerrantly inspired by God. That we must believe the blood of our Lord shed in a substitutionary death placates an offended deity. That we must believe in the second coming of our Lord. Imagine — having to believe that Jesus died to save sinners in order to be a minister. Fosdick was horrified.

As we celebrate the centennial of this infamous sermon, I want to ask: one hundred years later, have the fundamentalists won?

## What Is a Win?

A win is a church that is built on the solid rock of Jesus Christ, the Son of the Living God. Nothing short of that is a win.

Jesus asks the greatest question here in Matthew 16 verse 13: *Who do men say that I, the Son of Man, am?* That is the question before all of humanity. And there are at least four answers we find in the New Testament.

**Answer 1: An imposter.**

The Pharisees' answer. These were religious leaders who said: this man is a dangerous deceiver. He preaches grace unto salvation; we preach good works unto salvation. He consorts with sinners and hypocrites. He must be dealt with. This view was shared by Voltaire, who believed Jesus was a deceiver who gained power for himself. And at the foundation of Karl Marx's communism is the claim that Jesus Christ was an imposter. Be careful with the ideas you toy with.

**Answer 2: A great teacher.**

Nicodemus came to Jesus by night and said: *Rabbi, we know that you are a teacher come from God.* At that point Jesus was, for Nicodemus, nothing more than a great teacher. This is the answer of the world today — people are willing to say Jesus was a good man whose teachings are worth following. It is my father's answer. It is the answer of Buddhism and Confucianism. And it is the answer of the mainline denominations — the PCUSA, the United Methodist Church, the ELCA.

But I hope you can see this is impossible. Jesus cannot simply be a great teacher. He can be an imposter. He can be a lunatic. Or he can be exactly who he says he is. But he cannot be merely a great teacher, because he claims to be something else. A great teacher does not say: I am the way, the truth, and the life; no one comes to the Father except through me.

**Answer 3: A prophet.**

The masses who followed Jesus thought he might be Elijah, or John the Baptist, or Jeremiah, or one of the prophets. They were not willing to recognize the fullness of who he was, but they were willing to give him some kind of prophetic status. This is the answer of the cults and false religions today. Mormonism: Jesus is an angel from God. Islam: Jesus is a prophet of Allah. Jehovah's Witnesses: Jesus is a created being indwelt by the power of God. None of them are willing to say: Jesus is the Son of God, the Messiah.

**Answer 4: "You are the Christ, the Son of the living God."**

This is Peter's answer, speaking on behalf of the apostles. And consider who is giving this answer. These men had spent three years in constant, unparalleled intimacy with Jesus — witnessing every miracle, hearing every teaching, seeing every interaction. After all that intimate contact, the only answer they could give was: you are the Christ, the Son of the living God. You want evidence for who Jesus is? Here is your evidence: those who knew him best, could not say anything less.

## The Church Is Built on This Confession

Jesus' response is remarkable. He tells Peter: flesh and blood has not revealed this to you — not human wisdom, not religious experience, not philosophical deduction — but my Father who is in heaven. This is a divinely inspired statement.

And it is on this truth — and on this confession — that the church is built. Verse 18: *And on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades shall not prevail against it.*

Fosdick was arguing for an institution tolerant of all ideas concerning the Bible and concerning Christ. Jesus says: whatever that is that Fosdick was arguing for — that is not the church. Remove the Bible as the inspired and infallible Word of God; remove Jesus Christ as the Savior of the world — you do not have a church. You may have the synagogue of Satan. But you do not have a church.

And notice the most offensive claim Jesus makes: I am the way, the truth, and the life; no one comes to the Father except through me. People are comfortable with Jesus being *a* way. They are comfortable with him being an option. But that is not what he says. He says: embrace me as I reveal myself in Scripture, or face the wrath of God for your sins for eternity. Not one among many. The only one. That is the most offensive claim of Christianity.

## The Church Cannot Be Defeated

Hades — the underworld, standing for death and the dominion of Satan — has been defeated in the resurrection of Christ. As Paul says in 1 Corinthians 15: *Death is swallowed up in victory. O death, where is your sting? O Hades, where is your victory? The sting of death is sin, and the strength of sin is the law. But thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.*

Not even the last enemy will stand in the way of Jesus Christ building his church. The religion of Fosdick and the liberals is not Christianity, because without the Christ of the Bible, there is no Christianity.

## The Book of Providence

Now let me return to the question: have the fundamentalists won?

The aftermath of the modernist-fundamentalist controversy in the Presbyterian Church was that hundreds of ministers were kicked out from their pulpits and lost their pensions — and many lost their houses, because they lived in manses. Hundreds of churches lost their buildings, some after more than a century in them — because they stood for the Word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ.

The Senate of the Washington Presbytery of the PCUSA pronounced the following verdict against Roy Brumba, the first pastor of the Tacoma Bible Presbyterian Church, in November 1935. His crime? He had refused to support missionaries who denied the Bible and Jesus. He persuaded his church to only sponsor missionaries who affirm the Bible as the Word of God and Jesus Christ as the Savior that the Bible teaches. For this, the Presbytery said:

*Whereas you, Roy Brumba, have been convicted by sufficient proof of the sins of insubordination and violation of your ordination vows... by your sin and unfaithfulness have brought reproach on the cause of your master, we therefore do hereby depose and exclude you from the office of minister of the gospel, and prohibit you from exercising henceforth any of the powers and duties of that office.*

He was convicted of the sin of believing that pastors should believe that Jesus Christ rose from the dead.

In Collingswood, New Jersey, the congregation that would become Collingswood Bible Presbyterian Church left the building they had built and for months worshiped the Lord in a tent — in an empty lot. Representative of hundreds of churches.

From this perspective, it looks like the fundamentalists lost. The liberals successfully purged the PCUSA of those unwilling to compromise.

But there is more to the story. The daughter churches of the original fundamentalists have steadily grown in membership as they remain faithful to the Word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ. They have sent missionaries to the four corners of the world who started other churches that are vibrantly serving the Lord Jesus Christ. The PCUSA has seen its membership and influence decline drastically year after year. Its gospel of human goodness is driving its own members away.

So have the fundamentalists won?

Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the Living God. That is truth. He is building his church. Not even death and the powers of Satan will prevent him from doing so.

As long as faithful men and women stand for the Word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ — as long as Jesus is the sovereign master of the very church he is building — the only answer possible is a resounding yes. Jesus Christ is building his church, and the gates of hell will not prevail against her.

*Father, thank you that you are a God who builds your church. Thank you that the foundation is clear: Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the Living God. Enable us to remain faithful to that. And for those among us who do not yet see him as that — open their eyes, open their hearts, change their hearts, bring them to faith in the true God of the Bible, revealed to us in Jesus Christ who died for his church and came back to life for her. We pray these things in Jesus' name, amen.*
